hints.Hic: 'Hic' is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this man'
hints.Hic tamen: This is two words — only 'vivit' is the verb

hints.to conquer: 'to conquer' is 'vincere' — similar consonants but different verb

hints.Perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'vixit' — 'vivit' with present stem is present tense
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'vivat' — 'vivit' is indicative

hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see)
hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)
hints.venio, venire, veni, ventum: These are the parts of 'venire' (to come)

steps.prompt: What does 'Hic' refer to in context?

hints.The senate: The senate was just mentioned as understanding — 'Hic' points to the person being accused
hints.The consul: Cicero is the consul — 'Hic' refers to the person he's denouncing: Catiline
hints.The Roman people: 'Hic' is singular — it refers to one man, Catiline

steps.prompt: What does 'tamen' convey about the situation?
steps.target: Despite the senate knowing, Catiline still lives
choices: Catiline lives because of the senate
Despite the senate knowing, Catiline still lives

hints.Catiline lives because of the senate: 'tamen' means 'nevertheless' — it marks a contrast, not a cause
hints.Catiline barely survives: 'tamen' does not mean 'barely' — it means 'yet/nevertheless', expressing Cicero's outrage that Catiline is still alive and free
hints.The senate has pardoned Catiline: There is no pardon — 'tamen' expresses Cicero's frustration that despite knowledge of the conspiracy, Catiline remains alive

steps.prompt: What is the rhetorical effect of this extremely short sentence?
steps.target: Blunt shock — emphasizing the outrage with brevity
choices: It signals a transition to a new topic
Blunt shock — emphasizing the outrage with brevity
It shows Cicero running out of arguments
It is a standard legal formula

hints.It signals a transition to a new topic: Cicero continues on the same topic — the brevity intensifies the emotional impact
hints.It shows Cicero running out of arguments: The opposite — short sentences in oratory are deliberate and powerful
hints.It is a standard legal formula: This is not a legal formula — it's a rhetorically charged exclamation of disbelief
